narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.30—above the typical 3.21 average—and a strong LSAT score of 40, which exceeds the dataset average of 36.70, you've built an impressive academic foundation that positions you well above many peers. Your Year 1 and Year 3 school deciles both at 7 also reflect consistent performance in the upper range compared to averages around 5.5-5.7. Coming from an upper-middle-class family background further aligns with factors that often support success. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated your probability of failure at 43%, tipping the scales toward rejection for your law school application. This prediction hinges on a few key elements from your profile, with your planned part-time enrollment status standing out as the most influential factor—it significantly raised concerns because only about 7% of students opt for part-time, and the model associates this choice strongly with higher bar failure risk, pushing the prediction toward failure more than any other aspect. In contrast, your Year 3 decile rank worked strongly in your favor, pulling the odds toward passing by showing solid late-stage academic strength. Similarly, your undergraduate GPA provided a meaningful boost toward a passing prediction, countering some risks with its above-average quality. Compared to typical students in the dataset, your metrics are competitive overall, but the rarity and perceived challenges of part-time status outweighed these positives for this model. While this decision feels disappointing, especially given your strengths, it highlights how enrollment choices can overshadow even strong grades and test scores in bar passage forecasts. To move forward, consider reapplying with a full-time plan, as that aligns with the 93% majority and could flip the prediction—perhaps gather data on part-time success stories or boost other edges like references. You might also explore schools with flexible programs or appeal by emphasizing your unique circumstances. With your profile, targeted adjustments could open doors soon.
